energy/energy-stores-systems.summary: Describe energy stores and identify how energy is transferred between stores in different systems.

quiz.q: A ball is thrown upwards and slows down. Which energy transfer is occurring?
opts: Kinetic store → gravitational potential store — the ball slows as it gains height
Gravitational potential store → kinetic store — the ball is losing height
Chemical store → kinetic store — energy from muscles is still being converted
Thermal store → kinetic store — air warms the ball and pushes it up

wrong_explanations.1: Gravitational PE → kinetic occurs when the ball FALLS. While RISING, the ball slows (losing KE) and gains height (gaining GPE): kinetic → GPE.
wrong_explanations.2: Once released from the hand, the chemical energy of muscles is no longer involved — the transfer is purely kinetic → gravitational PE while rising.
wrong_explanations.3: Air resistance transfers some KE to thermal, but the dominant transfer while rising is kinetic → gravitational PE.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an energy TRANSFER PATHWAY, not an energy store?
opts: Heating — the process of thermal energy flowing from a hot to a cold object
Thermal energy — energy stored in the random motion of particles
Chemical energy — energy stored in chemical bonds of fuel
Nuclear energy — energy stored in atomic nuclei

wrong_explanations.1: Thermal energy IS an energy store — energy held in the random kinetic and potential energy of particles. Heating is the PATHWAY by which thermal energy moves.
wrong_explanations.2: Chemical energy is a store — energy held in molecular bonds, released when bonds break in reactions.
wrong_explanations.3: Nuclear energy is a store — energy held in atomic nuclei, released in fission or fusion.

foundation.common_mistake: STORES are where energy is held (kinetic, chemical, thermal etc.). PATHWAYS are how it moves (heating, mechanical work, electrical work, radiation). 'Heat' is a transfer pathway — not a store. You store THERMAL energy, not 'heat'.

energy/changes-in-energy.summary: Calculate kinetic energy, gravitational potential energy and elastic potential energy.

quiz.q: An 800 kg car travels at 20 m/s. What is its kinetic energy?
opts: 8000 J — forgot to square the speed: ½ × 800 × 20 = 8000
16,000 J — used speed not speed squared: ½ × 800 × 40

wrong_explanations.1: Must square the speed first: 20² = 400. Then ½ × 800 × 20 = 8000 — missing the squared step.
wrong_explanations.2: The ½ is essential in the kinetic energy formula — without it the answer doubles.
wrong_explanations.3: 16,000 comes from ½ × 800 × 40 — this would be the answer if v = √40, not v = 20.

quiz.q: A spring with k = 400 N/m is compressed 0.1 m. What elastic PE is stored?
opts: 4 J — forgot the ½: 400 × 0.01 = 4 J
20 J — forgot to square the extension: ½ × 400 × 0.1 = 20
40 J — forgot both ½ and to square: 400 × 0.1 = 40

wrong_explanations.1: The ½ is part of the formula: Ee = ½ke². Without ½: 400 × 0.01 = 4 J.
wrong_explanations.2: Extension must be squared first: 0.1² = 0.01. ½ × 400 × 0.1 = 20 is missing the squaring.
wrong_explanations.3: Both the ½ AND the squaring are missing: must be Ee = ½ × k × e².

foundation.common_mistake: In Ek = ½mv², SQUARE THE SPEED FIRST before multiplying. Write out v² before doing anything else. Also: mass in kg, speed in m/s. Convert 500 g → 0.5 kg and 72 km/h → 20 m/s before substituting.

steps: m = 5 kg, g = 9.8 N/kg, h = 3 m

energy/energy-changes-in-systems.summary: Use specific heat capacity to calculate energy changes when substances are heated or cooled.

quiz.q: A 2 kg iron block (c = 450 J/kg°C) cools from 150°C to 30°C. How much energy is released?
opts: 27,000 J — used final temperature (30°C) not temperature change (120°C)
135,000 J — used initial temperature (150°C) not temperature change
1350 J — divided instead of multiplied

wrong_explanations.1: Δθ = 150 − 30 = 120°C is the temperature CHANGE. Using 30°C gives ΔE = 2 × 450 × 30 = 27,000 J — using the final temperature, not the change.
wrong_explanations.2: Δθ is not the initial temperature — it is the CHANGE (150 − 30 = 120). 2 × 450 × 150 = 135,000 J is too large.
wrong_explanations.3: ΔE = m × c × Δθ requires multiplication — 2 × 450 × 120 = 108,000 J.

quiz.q: Why is water used in central heating systems rather than a cheaper liquid?
opts: Water has a very high SHC (4200 J/kg°C) — it carries large amounts of thermal energy per kg per °C rise, so less water is needed
Water has a low SHC — it heats up quickly so the system responds faster
Water is denser than most liquids — it carries more energy due to its mass
Water has zero SHC at 100°C — it conducts heat perfectly at boiling point

wrong_explanations.1: The opposite is true — water's HIGH SHC is the key property. A low SHC would mean it heats quickly but also loses heat quickly and carries less energy.
wrong_explanations.2: Water is less dense than many metals — density is not the primary reason it's used in heating systems.
wrong_explanations.3: SHC doesn't become zero at any temperature — SHC is a property of the material, roughly constant over normal temperature ranges.

foundation.common_mistake: Temperature change (Δθ) is final temperature MINUS initial temperature — not the final temperature alone. If water heats from 20°C to 60°C, Δθ = 40°C, not 60°C. Also: mass must be in kg — convert grams first (÷1000).

fifas.label: SHC Calculation
fifas.question: How much energy is needed to heat 0.5 kg of water from 25°C to 85°C? (c = 4200 J/kg°C)

energy/power.summary: Define power as rate of energy transfer and calculate power from energy and time.

quiz.q: A 2000 W heater is on for 3 minutes. How much energy does it transfer?
opts: 6000 J — used minutes not seconds: E = 2000 × 3
11.1 J — divided instead of multiplied: 2000 ÷ 180
6,000,000 J — multiplied by 3000 instead of 180

wrong_explanations.1: 3 minutes must be converted to seconds: 3 × 60 = 180 s. E = 2000 × 3 = 6000 J uses minutes, not seconds.
wrong_explanations.2: P = E/t → E = P × t, not P ÷ t.

quiz.q: Two students both lift a 10 kg bag 5 m. Student A takes 10 s, B takes 20 s. How do their power outputs compare?
opts: A has twice B's power — same work (mgh), but A does it in half the time
Both have the same power — they do the same total work
B has more power — taking longer means working harder overall
Cannot compare — they take different times so cannot use the same equation

wrong_explanations.1: Same work ÷ same time = same power — but the TIMES ARE DIFFERENT. P = W/t; A: P = mgh/10; B: P = mgh/20. A's power is twice B's.
wrong_explanations.2: More time for the same work = LESS power. B takes longer so B has lower power.
wrong_explanations.3: The same equation P = W/t applies to both — using different times is exactly how power differs between them.

foundation.common_mistake: Time MUST be in SECONDS when using P = E/t with energy in joules. '5 minutes' = 300 s, NOT 5. Forgetting to convert is the most common mistake in power calculations.

fifas.label: Power — Stair Climb
fifas.question: A 50 kg student climbs 4 m of stairs in 5 seconds. Calculate her power output. (g = 9.8 N/kg)
steps: P = W ÷ t, where W = m × g × h

energy/energy-transfers-in-a-system.summary: Describe how energy is conserved, dissipated and how unwanted transfers can be reduced.

quiz.q: A pendulum swings and eventually stops. Where has the energy gone?
opts: Dissipated to thermal stores of the surrounding air and pivot through air resistance and friction
The energy is destroyed — the pendulum stopping proves this
Stored in the pendulum's elastic potential energy store ready to restart
Converted to sound only, which then disappears

wrong_explanations.1: Energy is NEVER destroyed — this violates the law of conservation of energy. The pendulum slows because energy is dissipated, not destroyed.
wrong_explanations.2: Once stopped, the pendulum has no stored elastic PE — it is hanging at rest and any elastic PE would have been released.
wrong_explanations.3: Some sound is produced, but sound also eventually dissipates to thermal energy in the air. No energy disappears — all ends up as thermal energy of the surroundings.

quiz.q: Why does adding loft insulation reduce heating bills?
opts: It slows the rate of thermal energy transfer from the warm house to the cold surroundings — less energy needed to maintain temperature
It generates heat from the solar radiation it absorbs
It stores thermal energy during the day and releases it at night
It prevents cold draughts entering through the roof — stopping convection

wrong_explanations.1: Loft insulation works by reducing CONDUCTION through the ceiling — the fibres trap air (poor thermal conductor) and slow heat loss.
wrong_explanations.2: While insulation has thermal mass, this is not its primary mechanism — reducing the RATE of heat loss is.
wrong_explanations.3: Loft insulation does reduce some convection at the ceiling, but its primary mechanism is reducing thermal CONDUCTION.

foundation.common_mistake: Energy is NEVER destroyed — it is dissipated to less useful stores. 'Lost' energy has been transferred to the thermal energy of the surroundings — it is still there, just spread out and difficult to use again. Never say energy is 'used up' or 'gone'.

energy/efficiency.summary: Calculate efficiency as a decimal or percentage and interpret Sankey diagrams.

quiz.q: A device has efficiency 0.6 and is supplied with 500 J. How much energy is wasted?
opts: 300 J — this is the useful output, not the wasted amount
500 J — all energy is wasted at efficiency 0.6
833 J — dividing 500 ÷ 0.6

wrong_explanations.1: 300 J is the USEFUL output (60% of 500). WASTED = total − useful = 500 − 300 = 200 J.
wrong_explanations.2: At efficiency 0.6, only 40% (200 J) is wasted — not all 500 J.
wrong_explanations.3: 500 ÷ 0.6 = 833 J: this would be the input needed to get 500 J useful output, not the wasted energy.

quiz.q: An LED uses 11 J/s total and produces 10 J/s of light. A filament bulb produces the same 10 J/s of light but uses 100 J/s. How much more energy per second does the bulb waste?
opts: 89 J/s — LED wastes 1 J/s, bulb wastes 90 J/s; difference = 89 J/s
0 J/s — both produce the same useful light so waste must be equal
10 J/s — the useful output is 10 J/s so waste is also 10 J/s
100 J/s — the bulb uses 100 J/s so wastes all of it

wrong_explanations.1: Same USEFUL output does NOT mean same waste. LED: wastes 11 − 10 = 1 J/s. Bulb: wastes 100 − 10 = 90 J/s. Difference = 89 J/s.
wrong_explanations.2: Wasted = total − useful. The TOTAL inputs are very different (11 vs 100 J/s).
wrong_explanations.3: Bulb uses 100 J/s but 10 J/s is useful light — it wastes 90 J/s, not 100 J/s.

foundation.common_mistake: Efficiency = useful output ÷ TOTAL INPUT — not useful ÷ wasted. Result must be ≤ 1 (≤ 100%). If your answer exceeds 1, you have divided the wrong way. Wasted energy = total input − useful output (not the denominator).

fifas.label: Efficiency Calculation
fifas.question: A car engine uses 20,000 J of chemical energy and produces 7,000 J of useful kinetic energy. Calculate its efficiency.
steps: efficiency = useful output energy ÷ total input energy

energy/energy-resources.summary: Compare renewable and non-renewable energy resources, their uses and environmental impacts.

quiz.q: Which of the following energy resources is renewable?
opts: Geothermal — heat from inside the Earth is continuously produced and will not run out on human timescales
Nuclear — uranium can be recycled so supplies will not run out
Coal — new coal forms continuously from organic material
Natural gas — underground reserves are replenished by geological processes

wrong_explanations.1: Nuclear fuel is FINITE — while some can be reprocessed, uranium supplies will eventually run out. It is non-renewable.
wrong_explanations.2: Coal takes MILLIONS of years to form — it is not replenished on any human-relevant timescale. Non-renewable.
wrong_explanations.3: Natural gas reserves are being used far faster than they form — a finite, non-renewable resource.

quiz.q: Why are solar and wind described as 'intermittent' energy sources?
opts: They only generate electricity when the sun shines or wind blows — output is variable and cannot be guaranteed on demand
They are expensive to maintain and break down frequently
They produce too much electricity at peak times, requiring regular shutdown
They can only be used in specific countries, not globally

wrong_explanations.1: 'Intermittent' refers specifically to VARIABLE, WEATHER-DEPENDENT output — not maintenance reliability.
wrong_explanations.2: Overproduction is a grid-management challenge but 'intermittent' specifically means UNPREDICTABLE output dependent on weather, not overproduction.
wrong_explanations.3: Wind and solar are used globally — 'intermittent' describes the time-variability of output, not geographic limitation.

foundation.common_mistake: Bio-fuels ARE renewable — the plants regrow. Nuclear is NOT renewable — uranium is finite. Hydroelectric, wind, tidal and wave are all renewable. Also: 'renewable' does NOT mean 'zero environmental impact' — every resource has some impact.

electricity/circuit-symbols.summary: Draw and interpret circuit diagrams using standard symbols.

quiz.q: How should an ammeter be connected to measure the current through a bulb?
opts: In series with the bulb — in the same loop so the same current flows through both
In parallel with the bulb — bridging across it so it measures the voltage
Anywhere in the circuit — ammeters measure the total current of the whole circuit
Between the two terminals of the battery — to measure the source current

wrong_explanations.1: In parallel is how you connect a VOLTMETER, not an ammeter. An ammeter in parallel would short-circuit the component.
wrong_explanations.2: An ammeter must be in series specifically with the component being measured — position matters in branched circuits.
wrong_explanations.3: Placing an ammeter directly across the battery terminals would short-circuit the battery — dangerous and incorrect.

quiz.q: What does the symbol of a circle with a cross inside represent?
opts: A lamp (bulb) — the cross represents the filament inside the glass envelope
A motor — the cross indicates rotation
A fuse — the cross means it can break the circuit
An LED — the cross represents light emission

wrong_explanations.1: A motor symbol is a circle with M inside — not a cross.
wrong_explanations.2: A fuse symbol is a rectangle with a line through it — not a circle with a cross.
wrong_explanations.3: An LED symbol is a diode (triangle + line) with two outward arrows — not a circle with a cross.

foundation.common_mistake: Ammeters are connected IN SERIES — they must be in the same loop as the component. Voltmeters are connected IN PARALLEL — they bridge across the component. Swapping these will give wrong readings (and could damage the meter). Always draw with a ruler — freehand diagrams lose marks.

higher.common_mistake: Ammeters are connected IN SERIES — they must be in the same loop as the component. Voltmeters are connected IN PARALLEL — they bridge across the component. Swapping these will give wrong readings (and could damage the meter). Always draw with a ruler — freehand diagrams lose marks.

electricity/electrical-charge-current.summary: Describe electric current as flow of charge and calculate charge, current and time.

quiz.q: A charge of 180 C flows through a resistor in 2 minutes. What is the current?
opts: 90 A — I = 180 ÷ 2 (used minutes not seconds)
360 A — I = 180 × 2 (multiplied instead of divided)

wrong_explanations.1: 2 minutes = 120 seconds. I = 180 ÷ 2 = 90 A — used minutes, not seconds.
wrong_explanations.2: I = Q ÷ t, not Q × t. Must divide.

quiz.q: In a series circuit, an ammeter before the first lamp reads 0.3 A. What does an ammeter between the two lamps read?
opts: 0.3 A — current is identical at every point in a series circuit
Less than 0.3 A — the first lamp has used some of the current
More than 0.3 A — the second lamp draws extra current
Depends on the resistance of each lamp

wrong_explanations.1: Current is NOT 'used up' — energy is transferred but the charges continue flowing. Current is identical throughout a series circuit.
wrong_explanations.2: Current cannot increase through a series circuit without a junction.
wrong_explanations.3: In a series circuit, current is always the same at all points regardless of individual resistances.

foundation.common_mistake: Time must be in SECONDS when using Q = It. If given minutes, multiply by 60 first. In a series circuit, current is the SAME at every point — it does not get 'used up' passing through components.

electricity/current-resistance-pd.summary: Apply Ohm's Law (V = IR) to calculate current, resistance and potential difference.

quiz.q: A 6 Ω resistor has 3 A through it. What is the potential difference?

wrong_explanations.1: R ÷ I gives Ω/A — not volts. V = I × R = 3 × 6 = 18 V.
wrong_explanations.2: I ÷ R gives A/Ω — not volts. The equation is V = I × R.
wrong_explanations.3: There is no averaging formula in Ohm's Law.

quiz.q: On an I–V graph for an ohmic conductor, what does a steeper gradient indicate?
opts: Lower resistance — more current per volt (I = V/R, larger I means smaller R)
Higher resistance — steeper means more opposition
Higher pd — the axes are swapped
Non-ohmic behaviour — only curved graphs are ohmic

wrong_explanations.1: Steeper I–V gradient means MORE current for same pd → LOWER resistance. High resistance = shallower gradient.
wrong_explanations.2: On a standard I–V graph (I on y-axis, V on x-axis), steeper = more current per volt = lower R.
wrong_explanations.3: Ohmic conductors produce straight-line I–V graphs — steepness indicates the R value.

foundation.common_mistake: R = V ÷ I, NOT V × I. Use the triangle: cover the unknown — the remaining two show the operation. 'Voltage' is acceptable in everyday speech but the precise term is POTENTIAL DIFFERENCE.

electricity/resistors.summary: Describe I–V characteristics of resistors, lamps, diodes, thermistors and LDRs.

quiz.q: When temperature rises, what happens to a thermistor's resistance and the current through it?
opts: Resistance decreases, current increases — NTC thermistors have lower resistance at higher temperatures
Resistance increases, current decreases — hotter means more resistance like a metal wire
Resistance stays the same — thermistors are ohmic
Current stays constant — current is set by the battery

wrong_explanations.1: Metal wires do increase resistance with temperature, but thermistors are NTC — opposite behaviour.
wrong_explanations.2: Thermistors are NOT ohmic — resistance changes significantly with temperature.
wrong_explanations.3: I = V/R — if R changes, I changes too (assuming constant pd).

quiz.q: An LDR is in series with a fixed resistor and battery. A light is switched on. What happens to the current?
opts: Current increases — LDR resistance falls in light, total circuit resistance falls, current rises
Current decreases — more light makes the LDR resist more
Current stays the same — the fixed resistor controls the current
Current becomes zero — LDR blocks current in light

wrong_explanations.1: LDR resistance DECREASES in light. Lower total resistance → I = V/R gives higher current.
wrong_explanations.2: Fixed resistor limits the minimum resistance but total R still falls when LDR R falls.
wrong_explanations.3: LDRs are not diodes — they conduct in both directions, just with different resistance values.

foundation.common_mistake: Thermistor resistance DECREASES with higher temperature (NTC). LDR resistance DECREASES with more light. Both are the OPPOSITE of what students often assume. A filament lamp is NOT ohmic — its resistance increases as it heats up.

electricity/series-parallel-circuits.summary: Calculate current, potential difference and resistance in series and parallel circuits.

quiz.q: Two lamps are connected in parallel across a 6 V battery. What is the pd across each lamp?
opts: 6 V each — every branch in a parallel circuit has the same pd as the supply
3 V each — the pd splits equally between the two parallel lamps
12 V each — parallel doubles the voltage per branch
Depends on lamp resistance — cannot determine without values

wrong_explanations.1: Pd splitting happens in SERIES. In PARALLEL, all branches have the SAME pd as the supply.
wrong_explanations.2: Parallel circuits never increase voltage beyond the supply.
wrong_explanations.3: In parallel, all branches have the supply pd regardless of resistance — only the branch CURRENT changes with resistance.

quiz.q: Why is household wiring parallel rather than series?
opts: Each appliance gets full mains voltage and works independently — switching one off doesn't affect others
Parallel uses less total current so it is safer
Series cannot carry enough current for multiple appliances
Parallel wiring is cheaper to install

wrong_explanations.1: Parallel draws MORE total current (I_total = sum of branches) — safety comes from fuses, not lower current.
wrong_explanations.2: Series wiring would mean each appliance gets a reduced share of 230 V and all would stop if one broke.
wrong_explanations.3: Cost is a secondary concern — the electrical reasons (full voltage, independent operation) are the key ones.

foundation.common_mistake: In PARALLEL, total resistance is LESS than any individual resistance — more branches = more paths = lower resistance. Adding resistors in SERIES always increases total resistance. Students often mix these up.

fifas.question: R₁ = 3 Ω and R₂ = 7 Ω in series with a 20 V battery. Find the current and pd across each resistor.
steps: R_total = R₁ + R₂; I = V ÷ R_total; V₁ = I × R₁; V₂ = I × R₂
I = 2 A throughout; V₁ = 6 V; V₂ = 14 V (check: 6 + 14 = 20 V ✓)

electricity/direct-alternating-pd.summary: Distinguish dc and ac, state UK mains values and read oscilloscope traces.

quiz.q: An oscilloscope shows a horizontal line above the zero axis. What does this represent?
opts: A DC supply — constant pd in one direction
An AC supply — the line shows the average value of the alternating current
No current — horizontal means the circuit is off
Very high frequency AC — oscillations too fast to see

wrong_explanations.1: An AC trace is sinusoidal. A flat line means CONSTANT pd — that is DC. A line at zero means no pd; above zero means a positive DC voltage.
wrong_explanations.2: A horizontal line above zero means a CONSTANT positive pd — DC, not AC.
wrong_explanations.3: Very fast AC would appear blurred or as a thick band — a sharp flat line specifically indicates DC.

quiz.q: What are the frequency and voltage of the UK mains supply?
opts: 50 Hz and 230 V
60 Hz and 230 V — 60 Hz is the UK standard
50 Hz and 110 V — 110 V is the UK mains voltage
50 Hz and 325 V — 325 V is the correct value

wrong_explanations.1: 60 Hz is used in the USA — UK is 50 Hz.
wrong_explanations.2: 110 V is used in the USA — UK mains is ~230 V.
wrong_explanations.3: 325 V is the PEAK voltage — 230 V is the quoted rms (effective) value.

foundation.common_mistake: UK mains is 50 Hz — not 60 Hz (USA). The quoted 230 V is an rms value — the peak voltage is higher (~325 V). A DC trace is a flat line; an AC trace is a wave.

electricity/mains-electricity.summary: Describe three-core cable colour coding, the earth wire, fuses and electrical safety.

quiz.q: Why must a fuse be in the live wire, not the neutral wire?
opts: If in the neutral wire, the appliance remains at 230 V even when the fuse blows — still dangerous
The neutral wire has too much resistance for a fuse to work
Fuses only work with DC — neutral carries AC
The neutral wire is at 230 V — it would blow constantly

wrong_explanations.1: Neutral wire resistance is very low — fuses work electrically in either wire. The reason is SAFETY ISOLATION.
wrong_explanations.2: Both wires carry AC — fuses work with AC. The reason is about isolating the appliance from the dangerous 230 V.
wrong_explanations.3: Neutral is at ~0 V, not 230 V. Live is the dangerous wire at high pd.

quiz.q: A metal-cased appliance develops a fault — live touches the case. What happens if it is properly earthed?
opts: Large current flows through earth wire, fuse blows, circuit breaks — case is made safe
Current stays in the case — metal is a poor conductor
Earth wire permanently absorbs the fault current
Nothing — earth wire only activates in thunderstorms

wrong_explanations.1: Metal is an excellent conductor — current flows easily through it.
wrong_explanations.2: Earth wire provides a low-resistance path. The resulting surge blows the fuse. Earth does not permanently carry current.
wrong_explanations.3: Earth wire activates whenever live connects to earth (e.g. through the metal case) — not only in storms.

foundation.common_mistake: Earth wire normally carries NO current — only activates in a fault. Neutral wire (blue) IS at ~0 V but CAN carry current. Fuses and switches must be in the LIVE wire so the appliance is fully isolated when switched off.

electricity/power-electricity.summary: Calculate electrical power using P = VI and P = I²R, and select appropriate fuse ratings.

quiz.q: A resistor (R = 8 Ω) carries 3 A. What is the power dissipated?
opts: 24 W — P = I × R = 3 × 8 (forgot to square I)
576 W — P = I² × R² = 9 × 64 (squared both)

wrong_explanations.1: P = I × R gives volts (A × Ω = V) — not watts. Must square the current.
wrong_explanations.2: Only CURRENT is squared: P = I²R = 9 × 8 = 72 W.
wrong_explanations.3: R ÷ I gives Ω/A — not watts. P = I²R.

quiz.q: A 1380 W iron on 230 V mains. Which fuse?
opts: 13 A — I = 1380 ÷ 230 = 6 A; next rating above 6 A is 13 A
3 A — safest choice for any appliance
5 A — just below 6 A so it will work
1 A — smallest fuse is always safest

wrong_explanations.1: A 3 A fuse would blow immediately — the iron draws ~6 A.
wrong_explanations.2: 5 A is BELOW the 6 A operating current — it would blow under normal use.
wrong_explanations.3: 1 A is far below operating current — would blow immediately.

foundation.common_mistake: In P = I²R, CURRENT is squared, not resistance. Students often write P = I × R² by mistake. To find I from power: use I = P ÷ V (not P = I²R unless R is known and V is not).

electricity/energy-transfers-appliances.summary: Calculate energy transferred using E = Pt, use kWh and describe appliance energy transfers.

quiz.q: A 2 kW heater runs for 3 hours. How much energy does it use and what does it cost at 25p/kWh?
opts: 6000 kWh — used watts not kilowatts: 2000 × 3 = 6000
0.67 kWh — divided instead of multiplied
6 kWh and 25p — cost = 1 unit regardless of amount

wrong_explanations.1: Must use KILOWATTS: 2 kW × 3 h = 6 kWh. Using 2000 W gives 6000 — wrong unit.
wrong_explanations.2: Energy = P × t = 2 kW × 3 h = 6 kWh. Must multiply.
wrong_explanations.3: Cost = energy × price per unit = 6 × 25p = 150p = £1.50.

quiz.q: A 200 W fridge runs continuously. A 2000 W kettle runs 5 min/day. Which uses more energy in 24 hours?
opts: The fridge — 200 W × 24 h = 4.8 kWh vs kettle: 2 kW × (5/60) h ≈ 0.17 kWh
The kettle — much higher power
They use the same
Cannot compare without knowing brands

wrong_explanations.1: Higher power ≠ higher energy use — energy = power × TIME. Fridge runs 24 h; kettle runs only 5 min.
wrong_explanations.2: Energy = power × time. Fridge: 0.2 × 24 = 4.8 kWh. Kettle: 2 × (5/60) ≈ 0.17 kWh.
wrong_explanations.3: Power and time are all that's needed — brand is irrelevant.

foundation.common_mistake: For E = Pt in joules, time must be in SECONDS. For kWh, use kW and hours. Mixing units is the most common error. High-power appliance used briefly can use LESS energy than a low-power one running for hours.

electricity/national-grid.summary: Explain how the National Grid transmits electricity efficiently and the role of transformers.

quiz.q: Why is electricity transmitted at high voltage across the National Grid?
opts: High voltage means lower current for the same power — lower current means far less I²R heating in cables
High voltage means higher current — more charge carriers reduce resistance
Cables are designed only for high voltage — they cannot carry low voltage
High voltage prevents cable corrosion in rain

wrong_explanations.1: P = IV — for same power, high V means LOW current. Lower current → I²R losses much smaller.
wrong_explanations.2: Cable design is for mechanical and safety reasons — efficiency is determined by current levels.
wrong_explanations.3: Voltage has no effect on corrosion — cables are protected by materials regardless of voltage.

quiz.q: A transformer has 4000 primary turns, 200 secondary turns, primary voltage 20,000 V. What is the secondary voltage?
opts: 100 V — incorrect ratio calculation
20,000 V — voltage unchanged through transformer

wrong_explanations.1: Using np/ns inverts the ratio: Vs = Vp × (ns/np) = 20,000 × (200/4000) = 1000 V — not ×20.
wrong_explanations.3: Transformers CHANGE the voltage — that is their purpose. Vs = Vp × (ns/np).

foundation.common_mistake: Transformers only work with AC — DC produces no output. High voltage reduces CURRENT (not power) — cable losses are proportional to I² so even a small current reduction gives large savings.

particle-model/density-of-materials.summary: Define density and calculate it from mass and volume for solids and liquids.

quiz.q: A liquid has mass 800 g and volume 1000 cm³. What is its density in g/cm³?
opts: 800,000 g/cm³ — multiplied instead of divided
1.0 g/cm³ — density of all liquids is always 1 g/cm³

wrong_explanations.1: ρ = V ÷ m inverts the formula — it gives cm³/g, not g/cm³. Always divide MASS by VOLUME.
wrong_explanations.2: Multiplying mass × volume gives g·cm³ — not a density unit.
wrong_explanations.3: Only WATER has density 1 g/cm³ — other liquids vary. This liquid (0.8 g/cm³) would float on water.

quiz.q: Why does ice float on water?
opts: Ice is less dense than liquid water — its particles are arranged in a more open lattice structure, giving lower density (917 kg/m³ vs 1000 kg/m³)
Ice is lighter than water because it is frozen — freezing reduces mass
Ice floats because it is a solid — all solids float on their liquid form
Ice has more volume than the same mass of water — it sinks if enough weight is added

wrong_explanations.1: Freezing doesn't change mass — the same molecules are present. Ice floats because of LOWER DENSITY, not lower mass.
wrong_explanations.2: Most solids are DENSER than their liquid form (e.g. solid iron sinks in liquid iron). Water/ice is a special exception.
wrong_explanations.3: Ice is less dense because its crystal structure spreads particles further apart — and because it's less dense, it floats. The last part of the option is correct (adding weight to ice can sink it) but irrelevant to why ice naturally floats.

foundation.common_mistake: Units for density are kg/m³ (SI) or g/cm³ (also common). Do NOT mix — if mass is in grams and volume in cm³, density comes out in g/cm³. Convert to kg/m³ by multiplying by 1000. Also: 1 cm³ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ m³.

particle-model/changes-of-state.summary: Describe changes of state in terms of particles and explain why mass is conserved.

quiz.q: 200 g of water is placed in a freezer and completely freezes. What is the mass of the ice formed?
opts: 200 g — mass is conserved during changes of state; no particles are created or destroyed
Less than 200 g — some water evaporates as it cools
More than 200 g — ice is larger in volume so it must be heavier
Depends on temperature — colder freezers produce heavier ice

wrong_explanations.1: In a sealed container with no evaporation, mass is perfectly conserved — but even with some evaporation, the PRINCIPLE is conservation of mass in a change of state.
wrong_explanations.2: Ice IS larger in volume than liquid water — but volume and mass are different. The MASS is the same; ice is simply less dense.
wrong_explanations.3: Temperature affects rate of freezing and final temperature, not the mass of ice formed.

quiz.q: Why is melting a physical change rather than a chemical change?
opts: No new substances are formed — the material's chemical identity is preserved and the change is reversible
Melting only affects the surface of the solid — the inside remains chemically unchanged
Physical changes always involve energy, while chemical changes do not
Melting is chemical because the structure of the material is permanently altered

wrong_explanations.1: Melting is a bulk process — the entire solid changes state, not just the surface.
wrong_explanations.2: Both chemical and physical changes can involve energy. The distinction is whether new chemical substances are formed.
wrong_explanations.3: The ARRANGEMENT of particles changes (structure), but the CHEMICAL IDENTITY doesn't — it's still the same compound. Chemical changes produce new substances.

foundation.common_mistake: Changes of state are PHYSICAL changes — not chemical. The substance remains the same chemical compound throughout. Mass is ALWAYS conserved in a change of state — the number of particles doesn't change, only their arrangement.

particle-model/internal-energy.summary: Define internal energy and explain how heating affects a system.

quiz.q: A substance is heated at constant power. Its temperature stays constant for several minutes. What is happening?
opts: A change of state — energy is increasing potential energy to break intermolecular bonds, not raising temperature
The heater has broken — no energy is being supplied
The substance has reached its maximum possible temperature
The substance is losing heat to the surroundings at exactly the same rate as energy is supplied

wrong_explanations.1: If no energy were being supplied, temperature would fall (cool to room temperature) — a constant temperature at the melting/boiling point specifically indicates a change of state.
wrong_explanations.2: There is no maximum temperature concept for most substances in normal conditions — temperature can always rise further if energy is supplied.
wrong_explanations.3: While heat loss to surroundings can cause apparent temperature plateaus, in exam context a flat section on a heating curve always indicates a change of state — the temperature is pinned at the melting or boiling point.

quiz.q: A large cool swimming pool and a small hot cup of tea — which has greater internal energy?
opts: The swimming pool — despite lower temperature, it has vastly more particles so its total KE + PE is much greater
The cup of tea — higher temperature means more internal energy
They have the same — internal energy depends only on temperature
Cannot compare — internal energy requires knowing density as well

wrong_explanations.1: Higher temperature means higher AVERAGE KE per particle — but internal energy is the TOTAL for all particles. The pool has far more particles, so total internal energy is greater.
wrong_explanations.2: Internal energy depends on temperature, mass AND specific heat capacity — not temperature alone.
wrong_explanations.3: Density is relevant to calculating particle numbers but the main point is that internal energy is an EXTENSIVE property — it scales with the amount of matter.

foundation.common_mistake: During a CHANGE OF STATE, temperature stays CONSTANT — energy goes into potential energy (breaking bonds), not kinetic energy. Students often think heating always raises temperature — but at melting/boiling point, temperature is flat on the heating curve until the change is complete.

particle-model/temperature-changes-shc.summary: Use ΔE = mcΔθ to calculate energy changes when substances are heated or cooled.

quiz.q: A 2 kg iron block (c = 450 J/kg°C) cools from 200°C to 50°C. How much energy is released?
opts: 45,000 J — used final temperature (50°C) not temperature change (150°C)
180,000 J — used initial temperature (200°C) not temperature change
900 J — ΔE = c × Δθ (forgot mass)

wrong_explanations.1: Δθ = 200 − 50 = 150°C (change, not final). Using 50°C: 2 × 450 × 50 = 45,000 J — wrong Δθ.
wrong_explanations.2: Using 200°C: 2 × 450 × 200 = 180,000 J — using initial temp, not the change.
wrong_explanations.3: Must include mass: ΔE = m × c × Δθ = 2 × 450 × 150 = 135,000 J.

quiz.q: Why is water used as the coolant in car engines rather than a cheaper fluid?
opts: Water's high SHC (4200 J/kg°C) means it absorbs large amounts of thermal energy per kg per °C — effective at cooling without a large temperature rise
Water has a low SHC — it heats up quickly so it removes heat from the engine faster
Water is electrically insulating — it prevents short circuits in the engine
Water has the highest density of any liquid — it flows faster through the cooling system

wrong_explanations.1: A LOW SHC would mean water heats up quickly and saturates with heat — counterproductive for cooling. HIGH SHC means it can absorb lots of heat before its temperature rises much.
wrong_explanations.2: Water is a conductor (especially tap water with dissolved ions) — electrical insulation is not the reason.
wrong_explanations.3: Water is not the densest liquid — mercury is far denser. Flow rate is determined by pump design, not density alone.

foundation.common_mistake: Δθ is FINAL minus INITIAL temperature — not the final temperature alone. Heating from 20°C to 60°C gives Δθ = 40°C, not 60°C. Also convert mass to kg (not grams) before substituting.

particle-model/specific-latent-heat.summary: Define specific latent heat and calculate energy for changes of state using E = mL.

quiz.q: How much energy is released when 0.5 kg of steam at 100°C condenses to water at 100°C? (Lv = 2,260,000 J/kg)
opts: 0 J — no energy change because temperature stays at 100°C
168,000 J — used SHC equation instead of latent heat

wrong_explanations.1: E = m × L = 0.5 × 2,260,000. Using 2 for mass gives E = 4,520,000 J — that's for 2 kg, not 0.5 kg.
wrong_explanations.2: Temperature staying constant does NOT mean no energy change. Energy is released as bonds FORM during condensation — latent heat is released, not absorbed.
wrong_explanations.3: For a change of state, use E = mL. The SHC equation (ΔE = mcΔθ) applies when TEMPERATURE CHANGES — here temperature is constant.

quiz.q: Why does sweating cool the body?
opts: Sweat evaporating absorbs latent heat of vaporisation from the skin — the energy taken from the skin during evaporation lowers its temperature
Sweat is cold — it cools the skin by conduction when applied
Sweat reflects sunlight — reducing energy absorbed by the skin
Sweat increases skin density — denser skin conducts heat away faster

wrong_explanations.1: Sweat is at body temperature (37°C) — it is not cold. The cooling comes from EVAPORATION absorbing latent heat.
wrong_explanations.2: Sweat is transparent — it doesn't reflect significant sunlight.
wrong_explanations.3: Skin density doesn't change meaningfully due to sweat — cooling is entirely an evaporative latent heat effect.

foundation.common_mistake: During a change of state, temperature does NOT change — the flat sections on a heating curve. E = mL has NO temperature change term — unlike ΔE = mcΔθ. Students often confuse the two equations. If temperature is changing, use ΔE = mcΔθ. If state is changing (temperature constant), use E = mL.

particle-model/particle-motion-pressure.summary: Explain gas pressure in terms of particle motion and describe how temperature and volume affect pressure.

quiz.q: Why does the pressure inside a tyre increase when a car has been driven for a while?
opts: The tyre gets hotter — higher temperature means faster-moving air molecules, more frequent and harder collisions with the tyre walls
Air leaks into the tyre from outside — more molecules means higher pressure
The tyre volume decreases as the car is driven — smaller volume means higher pressure
The tyre heats up and the air molecules get larger — more molecules hit the walls

wrong_explanations.1: Air doesn't leak IN to a sealed tyre — if anything, slow leaks go OUT. The pressure rises due to TEMPERATURE increase.
wrong_explanations.2: Tyre volume stays approximately constant while driving (unless there's a puncture). The pressure rises due to TEMPERATURE increase at constant volume.
wrong_explanations.3: Gas molecules don't change size with temperature — they just move FASTER, increasing collision frequency and force.

quiz.q: A gas is at 27°C and 100 kPa. It is heated to 327°C at constant volume. What is the new pressure?
opts: 200 kPa — T₁ = 300 K, T₂ = 600 K; pressure doubles as temperature doubles
1200 kPa — used Celsius values: 327 ÷ 27 × 100
50 kPa — temperature increased so pressure decreases
100 kPa — pressure doesn't change with temperature

wrong_explanations.1: Must convert to KELVIN: 27°C = 300 K, 327°C = 600 K. Ratio = 600/300 = 2. Using Celsius: 327/27 ≈ 12 — completely wrong ratio.
wrong_explanations.2: Pressure INCREASES when temperature increases at constant volume — more energetic collisions, higher pressure.
wrong_explanations.3: Pressure and temperature (in kelvin) are directly proportional at constant volume — higher temperature = higher pressure.

foundation.common_mistake: Temperature in gas law calculations MUST be in KELVIN — not Celsius. Add 273 to convert. 0°C = 273 K, NOT 0 K. Pressure is caused by COLLISIONS of molecules with the walls — not by the weight or speed alone, but by the RATE and FORCE of collisions.

fifas.label: Boyle's Law
fifas.question: A gas at 200 kPa occupies 3 m³. The gas is compressed to 1 m³ at constant temperature. Calculate the new pressure.
steps: p₁ = 200 kPa, V₁ = 3 m³, V₂ = 1 m³

atomic-structure/structure-of-atom.summary: Describe the structure of an atom including the nucleus, protons, neutrons and electrons.

quiz.q: Where is most of the mass of an atom concentrated?
opts: In the nucleus — protons and neutrons make up almost all the atomic mass; electrons are negligible
In the electron shells — electrons are the most numerous particles
Evenly spread throughout the atom — mass is uniformly distributed
On the outer electron shell — outermost electrons carry most of the mass

wrong_explanations.1: There are often as many electrons as protons, but each electron weighs ~1/1836 of a proton — their combined mass is negligible.
wrong_explanations.2: Mass is concentrated in the tiny nucleus — the rest of the atom is mostly empty space.
wrong_explanations.3: Outer electrons have even less mass than inner ones — all electron mass is negligible.

quiz.q: A neutral atom has 8 protons. How many electrons does it have?
opts: 8 — neutral atoms always have equal numbers of protons and electrons
16 — electrons come in pairs so there are twice as many
4 — only half the protons are balanced by electrons
0 — electrons are not part of the neutral atom

wrong_explanations.1: Electrons don't automatically pair — the number equals the number of protons for charge balance.
wrong_explanations.2: Only half the protons balanced would give the atom a net positive charge — neutral means equal numbers.
wrong_explanations.3: Electrons are an essential part of every atom — removing them creates a positive ion, not a neutral atom.

foundation.common_mistake: The nucleus contains protons AND neutrons — NOT electrons. Electrons orbit the nucleus in shells. The mass of an atom comes almost entirely from the nucleus (protons + neutrons) — electron mass is negligible.

atomic-structure/mass-number-isotopes.summary: Define atomic number, mass number and isotopes and interpret nuclear notation.

quiz.q: An atom has 17 protons and mass number 35. How many neutrons does it have?
opts: 18 — neutrons = mass number − atomic number = 35 − 17 = 18
35 — the mass number equals the neutron number
17 — same as the proton number
52 — neutrons = mass number + atomic number = 35 + 17

wrong_explanations.1: Mass number = protons + neutrons. So neutrons = 35 − 17 = 18. The mass number is NOT just neutrons.
wrong_explanations.2: Neutrons = proton number only when A = 2Z, which is not always the case.
wrong_explanations.3: Neutrons = A − Z = 35 − 17 = 18, not 35 + 17.

quiz.q: Two atoms have the same atomic number but different mass numbers. What are they?
opts: Isotopes — same element (same protons), different neutron count (different mass numbers)
Different elements — different mass numbers mean different elements
Ions — they have gained or lost electrons
Allotropes — different structural forms of the same element

wrong_explanations.1: ATOMIC NUMBER defines the element — not mass number. Same atomic number = same element = isotopes.
wrong_explanations.2: Ions differ in electron count — mass number and atomic number are unchanged when ions form.
wrong_explanations.3: Allotropes (like diamond and graphite) are different structural arrangements of atoms — not related to atomic/mass numbers.

foundation.common_mistake: Mass number is protons + NEUTRONS — not protons alone. To find neutrons: subtract atomic number FROM mass number. Isotopes have the same atomic number (same element) but different mass numbers (different neutron counts).

wrong_explanations.1: ATOMIC NUMBER defines the element — not mass number. Same atomic number = same element = isotopes.
wrong_explanations.2: Ions differ in electron count — mass number and atomic number are unchanged when ions form.
wrong_explanations.3: Allotropes (like diamond and graphite) are different structural arrangements of atoms — not related to atomic/mass numbers.

higher.common_mistake: Mass number is protons + NEUTRONS — not protons alone. To find neutrons: subtract atomic number FROM mass number. Isotopes have the same atomic number (same element) but different mass numbers (different neutron counts).

fifas.label: Particle Counts
fifas.question: An atom has nuclear notation ³⁵₁₇Cl. How many protons, neutrons and electrons does it have?
steps: Protons = atomic number (Z); Neutrons = A − Z; Electrons = protons (neutral atom)

atomic-structure/development-atomic-mode.summary: Describe how the model of the atom developed from solid sphere to nuclear model.

quiz.q: In Rutherford's alpha scattering experiment, most alpha particles passed straight through the gold foil. What does this tell us?
opts: The atom is mostly empty space — alpha particles encounter nothing to deflect them
Alpha particles are too fast to be deflected — their speed carries them through
Gold atoms have no charge — nothing to repel the positive alpha particles
The gold foil was too thin — a thicker foil would have deflected all particles

wrong_explanations.1: Speed could be reduced by using slower particles — the KEY conclusion is about structure, not speed.
wrong_explanations.2: The FEW particles that DID deflect show gold nuclei ARE positively charged — most pass through the empty space.
wrong_explanations.3: Thicker foil would increase the probability of deflection, but the fundamental conclusion — mostly empty space — remains.

quiz.q: Why did the results of alpha scattering disprove the plum pudding model?
opts: The plum pudding model predicted only small deflections — the large deflections and back-scattering showed the positive charge must be concentrated in a tiny nucleus
The plum pudding model predicted all particles would bounce back — but most passed through
The plum pudding model had no electrons — but the experiment detected electrons
The plum pudding model was proposed after the scattering experiment

wrong_explanations.1: The plum pudding model predicted most particles would PASS THROUGH or have SMALL deflections (spread-out positive charge) — NOT that all would bounce back.
wrong_explanations.2: The plum pudding model DID include electrons — Thomson discovered them and built them into the model.
wrong_explanations.3: Thomson proposed the plum pudding model in 1904 — BEFORE Rutherford's 1909 scattering experiment.

foundation.common_mistake: The alpha scattering experiment DISPROVED the plum pudding model — it did NOT confirm it. The fact that MOST particles passed straight through shows atoms are mostly empty space. The FEW that bounced back showed the nucleus is small and dense.

atomic-structure/radioactive-decay.summary: Describe alpha, beta and gamma radiation, their properties and uses and dangers.

quiz.q: Why is alpha radiation the most dangerous type when a source is inside the body?
opts: Alpha is highly ionising — it causes dense ionisation damage to nearby cells and cannot escape the body to be detected
Alpha has the highest energy of all three types — more energy means more damage
Alpha moves fastest — it collides more often with cells
Alpha is most penetrating — it reaches all organs from a single source

wrong_explanations.1: Alpha does not have the highest energy — gamma photons have very high energies too. The danger is from DENSE IONISATION in a small area.
wrong_explanations.2: Alpha is actually the SLOWEST of the three — and its high mass means it interacts strongly with matter.
wrong_explanations.3: Alpha is the LEAST penetrating — stopped by a few cm of air. That's exactly why it's so dangerous inside the body — all ionisation is deposited nearby.

quiz.q: A Geiger counter measures 340 Bq from a radioactive source. What does this mean?
opts: 340 nuclei in the source are decaying every second, each emitting radiation
The source has 340 radioactive atoms remaining in total
The source emits 340 joules of energy every second
The source has been decaying for 340 seconds

wrong_explanations.1: Activity = total number of radioactive atoms remaining, not the rate. Bq measures the RATE of decay (per second).
wrong_explanations.2: Bq measures rate in decays per second — not joules. Energy per decay is measured in eV or joules, not Bq.
wrong_explanations.3: Bq is a rate (per second) — not a duration.

foundation.common_mistake: Alpha is the MOST ionising but LEAST penetrating. Gamma is the LEAST ionising per path length but MOST penetrating. These are often confused. Alpha is most dangerous INSIDE the body; gamma is most dangerous OUTSIDE the body.

atomic-structure/nuclear-equations.summary: Write and balance nuclear equations for alpha and beta decay.

quiz.q: Thorium-234 (²³⁴₉₀Th) undergoes beta decay. What is the daughter nucleus?
opts: ²³⁴₉₁Pa (protactinium) — mass number unchanged, atomic number increases by 1
²³⁰₈₈Ra (radium) — mass number decreases by 4, atomic number decreases by 2
²³⁴₈₉Ac (actinium) — atomic number decreases by 1
²³⁸₉₂U (uranium) — mass number increases

wrong_explanations.1: That describes ALPHA decay (A−4, Z−2) — not beta decay.
wrong_explanations.2: Beta decay INCREASES atomic number by 1 — not decreases it.
wrong_explanations.3: Beta decay doesn't increase mass number — it is always unchanged. Z goes from 90 to 91.

quiz.q: In a nuclear equation, the mass numbers on both sides must balance. A nucleus of ²³⁸₉₂U emits an alpha particle. What is the mass number of the daughter nucleus?
opts: 234 — 238 − 4 = 234 (alpha carries away mass number 4)
236 — 238 − 2 = 236 (subtracting atomic number instead of mass number)
242 — 238 + 4 = 242 (added instead of subtracted)
238 — mass number doesn't change in alpha decay

wrong_explanations.1: The 2 from the atomic number change is subtracted from the bottom number — alpha takes away mass number 4 and atomic number 2.
wrong_explanations.2: Mass number cannot increase in alpha decay — the alpha particle carries 4 units of mass number away.
wrong_explanations.3: Mass number DOES change in alpha decay — it decreases by 4.

foundation.common_mistake: In beta decay, the MASS NUMBER stays the same — a neutron turns into a proton plus an electron. The atomic number INCREASES by 1 (one more proton). Students often think beta decay is like alpha and reduces mass number.

fifas.label: Alpha Decay Equation
fifas.question: Write the nuclear equation for alpha decay of ²²⁶₈₈Ra (radium).
steps: Alpha decay: A decreases by 4, Z decreases by 2. Product + ⁴₂He

atomic-structure/half-lives.summary: Define half-life, calculate remaining activity and explain the random nature of decay.

quiz.q: A radioactive source has a half-life of 4 hours and initial activity 640 Bq. What is the activity after 12 hours?
opts: 160 Bq — only 2 half-lives calculated (8 hours, not 12)
320 Bq — only 1 half-life applied
0 Bq — the source has fully decayed after 12 hours

wrong_explanations.1: 12 ÷ 4 = 3 half-lives. After 2: 640 → 320 → 160 (only 8 hours). After 3: → 80 Bq.
wrong_explanations.2: After 1 half-life (4 h): 640 → 320. After 2 (8 h): → 160. After 3 (12 h): → 80.
wrong_explanations.3: Radioactive sources never fully reach zero — activity halves each half-life but never reaches exactly zero (exponential decay).

quiz.q: Why is the half-life of a radioactive isotope described as a 'constant'?
opts: It always takes the same time for the activity to halve, regardless of temperature, pressure or how much has already decayed
It stays constant because the number of atoms in the sample doesn't change
It is constant because decay rate increases to compensate as fewer atoms remain
It is constant only at room temperature — at high temperatures the half-life changes

wrong_explanations.1: The number of undecayed atoms DOES decrease — but the proportion that decay per unit time stays fixed, so the time to halve is always the same.
wrong_explanations.2: If rate increased to compensate, activity would stay constant — but it falls exponentially, halving each half-life.
wrong_explanations.3: Half-life is independent of temperature, pressure, or chemical state — this is one of the key properties of radioactive decay.

foundation.common_mistake: After each half-life, the activity halves AGAIN from its current value — not from the original. After 3 half-lives starting at 1000 Bq: 500 → 250 → 125 Bq. Also: background radiation must be subtracted before half-life calculations.

fifas.label: Half-Life Calculation
fifas.question: A source has initial activity 960 Bq. Its half-life is 3 hours. What is the activity after 12 hours?
steps: Number of half-lives = total time ÷ half-life; remaining = initial × (½)ⁿ

atomic-structure/radioactive-contaminati.summary: Distinguish contamination from irradiation and explain the hazards and precautions for each.

quiz.q: A worker accidentally inhales radioactive dust containing an alpha emitter. Why is this particularly dangerous?
opts: Alpha is highly ionising — inside the body all the ionising energy is deposited in nearby lung tissue, causing severe local damage
Alpha travels far inside the body reaching all organs — more widespread damage
Alpha is easily exhaled — it escapes before causing harm
Alpha inside the body becomes beta radiation — which is more penetrating

wrong_explanations.1: Alpha has very SHORT range — it doesn't travel far. That's exactly the problem: all its energy is deposited locally in lung tissue.
wrong_explanations.2: Radioactive dust lodges in lung tissue and continues to emit radiation there — it doesn't simply get exhaled.
wrong_explanations.3: Radiation types don't change — alpha always remains alpha. Different isotopes emit different types, but the type doesn't transform.

quiz.q: A radiographer takes X-rays of patients all day. Which precaution best reduces their radiation dose?
opts: Leaving the room while the X-ray is taken — distance and a lead-lined wall reduces irradiation when not needed
Wearing a lead apron while staying in the room — lead blocks all radiation types
Taking fewer breaths during the X-ray — radiation enters through breathing
Washing hands after each X-ray — removes radiation from skin

wrong_explanations.1: A lead apron is useful protection but does not cover all body parts — leaving the room is more effective for reducing total dose.
wrong_explanations.2: X-rays are electromagnetic radiation — they are not inhaled. The concern is external irradiation, not contamination.
wrong_explanations.3: X-rays from the machine don't contaminate surfaces — the radiographer's dose comes from irradiation (external source), not contamination.

foundation.common_mistake: Contamination and irradiation are NOT the same. Contamination: radioactive material on/in you — source travels with you. Irradiation: external source — exposure stops when you move away. Alpha is most dangerous as INTERNAL contaminant (highly ionising, short range → all energy deposits locally).

forces/scalar-vector-quantities.summary: Distinguish scalar and vector quantities and represent vectors with arrows.

quiz.q: A runner completes one full lap of a 400 m circular track. What is the runner's distance and displacement?
opts: Distance = 400 m, displacement = 0 m — the runner returns to the starting point
Distance = 0 m, displacement = 400 m — same as the lap distance
Distance = 400 m, displacement = 400 m — both equal the lap length
Both are zero — one full lap cancels everything out

wrong_explanations.1: Displacement is the NET change in position. After a full lap the runner is back at start → displacement = 0. Distance is the total path = 400 m.
wrong_explanations.2: Distance = displacement only for straight-line motion in one direction — a full lap gives zero displacement.
wrong_explanations.3: Distance is never zero if the runner moved — it counts total path length regardless of direction.

quiz.q: Two forces act on a box: 8 N to the right and 3 N to the left. What is the resultant force?
opts: 5 N to the right — 8 − 3 = 5 N; direction is that of the larger force
11 N to the right — 8 + 3 = 11 N (added without considering direction)
5 N to the left — 3 − 8 = −5 N so leftward
Zero — two forces always cancel out

wrong_explanations.1: Vectors in OPPOSITE directions SUBTRACT. Adding gives 11 N which ignores direction.
wrong_explanations.2: The net force is in the direction of the LARGER force — 8 N is larger than 3 N so resultant is rightward.
wrong_explanations.3: Forces only cancel when equal in magnitude AND opposite in direction. 8 N ≠ 3 N so they don't cancel.

foundation.common_mistake: SPEED is scalar, VELOCITY is vector. DISTANCE is scalar, DISPLACEMENT is vector. The most common error is treating velocity and speed as the same thing. A car travelling in a circle at constant SPEED has changing VELOCITY (direction changes).

forces/contact-noncontact-forces.summary: Classify forces as contact or non-contact and give examples of each.

quiz.q: A book rests on a table. Which two forces act on the book and what type are they?
opts: Weight (gravity, non-contact, downward) and normal contact force (contact, upward) — these balance so the book is stationary
Weight (downward) and friction (upward) — friction stops the book from falling
Weight and tension — the table pulls the book down with tension
Only weight acts — no force is needed to keep a stationary object still

wrong_explanations.1: Friction acts parallel to surfaces (opposing sliding) — it doesn't act vertically on a stationary book sitting on a flat table.
wrong_explanations.2: Tension acts through strings/ropes — not through a solid table supporting a book.
wrong_explanations.3: Newton's First Law: a stationary object has balanced forces — weight down is balanced by normal contact force up.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a non-contact force?
opts: The gravitational pull of the Sun on the Earth — acts across empty space without touching
Air resistance on a falling skydiver — acts through contact with air molecules
Tension in a rope supporting a hanging mass — acts through physical contact along the rope
Upthrust on a submarine — acts through the water directly in contact with the hull

wrong_explanations.1: Air resistance is a CONTACT force — it acts through collisions between air molecules and the skydiver's body.
wrong_explanations.2: Tension is a CONTACT force — transmitted through the physical rope.
wrong_explanations.3: Upthrust is a CONTACT force — it acts through direct fluid pressure on the submerged surface.

foundation.common_mistake: Gravity is a NON-CONTACT force — it acts at a distance without objects touching. Weight is the gravitational force on an object. Normal contact force is NOT the same as weight — normal force is a REACTION from the surface, perpendicular to it.

forces/gravity.summary: Explain weight as gravitational force, distinguish mass from weight, and calculate using W = mg.

quiz.q: An astronaut has mass 80 kg. What is her weight on the Moon? (g_Moon = 1.6 N/kg)
opts: 784 N — W = 80 × 9.8 (used Earth's g)
50 kg — mass on the Moon is 1/6 of Earth mass
0 N — astronauts are weightless on the Moon

wrong_explanations.1: Use the Moon's gravitational field strength (1.6 N/kg), not Earth's (9.8 N/kg).
wrong_explanations.2: Mass NEVER changes — it is 80 kg on the Moon, on Earth, anywhere. Only weight changes.
wrong_explanations.3: The Moon has significant gravity (1.6 N/kg) — astronauts are not weightless on the surface. Weightlessness occurs in orbit (free fall).

quiz.q: A student says 'my weight is 60 kg'. What is wrong with this statement?
opts: Weight is measured in NEWTONS — kilograms measure MASS. Their weight is approximately 588 N (60 × 9.8)
Nothing is wrong — weight and mass are the same thing and can be measured in the same units
Weight should be in grams — 60,000 g
The statement is correct on the Moon but wrong on Earth

wrong_explanations.1: Weight and mass are different physical quantities — they cannot use the same units.
wrong_explanations.2: Grams are a unit of mass — not weight. Weight is always in newtons.
wrong_explanations.3: Weight is always in newtons everywhere — the statement would still be wrong on the Moon (just a different wrong value).

foundation.common_mistake: Mass and weight are DIFFERENT things. Mass (kg) is constant everywhere. Weight (N) changes with gravitational field strength. On the Moon, your MASS stays the same but your WEIGHT is less. Weight is measured in NEWTONS — not kilograms.

wrong_explanations.3: Weight is always in newtons everywhere — the statement would still be wrong on the Moon (just a different wrong value).

forces/resultant-forces.summary: Find the resultant of forces and relate it to the motion of an object.

quiz.q: A car travels at constant speed along a straight road. What does this tell us about the forces on the car?
opts: The resultant force is zero — driving force equals resistive forces (friction + air resistance)
No forces act on the car — it moves freely
The driving force is greater than resistive forces — the car is accelerating
The car must be slowing down — constant speed requires deceleration

wrong_explanations.1: Forces always act on a moving car — gravity, normal, driving force, air resistance. Constant speed means they BALANCE.
wrong_explanations.2: Constant speed means balanced forces (resultant = 0) — not acceleration. Acceleration requires UNBALANCED forces.
wrong_explanations.3: Constant speed is neither speeding up nor slowing down — it requires balanced forces.

quiz.q: A skydiver jumps from a plane. Initially they accelerate. Later they reach terminal velocity. Why do they stop accelerating?
opts: Air resistance increases with speed until it equals weight — resultant becomes zero, so acceleration stops
The skydiver runs out of energy from the jump — no more acceleration
Gravity decreases with altitude — weight reduces to zero at terminal velocity
Terminal velocity means constant acceleration, not constant speed

wrong_explanations.1: Skydivers don't 'run out of energy' — gravity continuously acts on them. Acceleration stops when forces BALANCE.
wrong_explanations.2: Gravity changes very little over the heights of a typical skydive — weight stays approximately constant.
wrong_explanations.3: Terminal velocity means CONSTANT SPEED (zero acceleration) — not constant acceleration.

foundation.common_mistake: A resultant force of ZERO does NOT mean the object is stationary — it means CONSTANT VELOCITY (which includes stationary). An object moving at constant speed in a straight line also has zero resultant force.

forces/work-done-energy-transfer.summary: Calculate work done by a force and understand it as energy transferred.

quiz.q: A person carries a 20 kg box horizontally for 10 m at constant height. How much work does gravity do on the box?
opts: 0 J — gravity acts downward but the box moves horizontally; no displacement in the direction of the gravitational force
196 J — W = mg = 20 × 9.8 (forgot to include distance)

wrong_explanations.1: W = F × s requires the displacement to be in the DIRECTION of the force. Gravity acts downward; box moves horizontally → no work done by gravity.
wrong_explanations.2: Same error — using horizontal distance for a vertical force.
wrong_explanations.3: mg gives weight (force) not work — must multiply by distance IN THE DIRECTION of the force, which is zero here.

quiz.q: A 400 N force does 3200 J of work. How far does the object move?

wrong_explanations.1: s = W × F multiplies — but W = Fs means s = W ÷ F.
wrong_explanations.2: s = F ÷ W inverts the rearrangement: s = W ÷ F = 3200 ÷ 400 = 8 m.
wrong_explanations.3: Adding work and force gives meaningless units (J + N). Use W = Fs rearranged: s = W ÷ F.

foundation.common_mistake: Work is only done when the object moves IN THE DIRECTION of the force. Holding a heavy weight stationary does NO work (no displacement) — even though it feels tiring. Also: W in this equation is work done (joules), NOT weight.

forces/forces-elasticity.summary: Apply Hooke's Law to springs and distinguish elastic from inelastic deformation.

quiz.q: A spring of natural length 10 cm is stretched to 16 cm by a 3 N force. What is the spring constant?
opts: 18.75 N/m — k = F/total length = 3/0.16
300 N/m — k = F/e = 3/0.01 (used 1 cm extension)

wrong_explanations.1: Extension is NOT the total length — it is the INCREASE: 16 − 10 = 6 cm. k = 3/0.16 uses total length incorrectly.
wrong_explanations.2: k = F/e, not e/F. 0.06/3 gives m/N — not N/m.
wrong_explanations.3: Extension = 6 cm = 0.06 m, not 0.01 m (1 cm). k = 3/0.06 = 50 N/m.

quiz.q: Why do you need to pull both ends of a rubber band to stretch it?
opts: Applying only one force would make the rubber band accelerate — two equal opposite forces are needed to deform it without moving it
One force is not strong enough — two forces together provide double the stretching force
Rubber bands only deform with two forces — a single force has no effect on elastic materials
The rubber band needs forces from both sides to stay in equilibrium while stretching

wrong_explanations.1: Two forces DON'T double the stretching — you still only apply one extension. The reason is Newton's First Law — a single force would cause acceleration, not deformation.
wrong_explanations.2: A single force CAN stretch an elastic material if one end is fixed — but then the fixed end provides the second force via Newton's Third Law.
wrong_explanations.3: Equilibrium (resultant = 0) IS the right idea here — for deformation without acceleration, net force on the object must be zero.

foundation.common_mistake: Extension (e) is NOT the total length of the spring — it is the INCREASE in length from the natural (unstretched) length. If a 10 cm spring stretches to 14 cm, e = 4 cm = 0.04 m.

forces/distance-speed-velocity.summary: Define and calculate distance, displacement, speed and velocity.

quiz.q: A cyclist rides 600 m east in 60 s, then 600 m west in 60 s. What is the average speed and average velocity for the whole journey?
opts: Speed = 10 m/s, velocity = 0 m/s — total distance = 1200 m, total time = 120 s; displacement = 0
Speed = 0 m/s, velocity = 10 m/s — velocity and speed are the same thing
Both 10 m/s — displacement equals total distance
Speed = 5 m/s, velocity = 5 m/s — average of 600 m in 60 s both ways

wrong_explanations.1: Speed and velocity are NOT the same — speed is scalar (total distance/time), velocity is vector (displacement/time).
wrong_explanations.2: Displacement = net change in position = 0 (back at start). Speed uses total distance (1200 m), velocity uses displacement (0 m).
wrong_explanations.3: Speed = total distance / total time = 1200/120 = 10 m/s, not 5 m/s.

quiz.q: A car drives around a roundabout at constant speed of 10 m/s. Is its velocity constant?
opts: No — direction changes continuously around the roundabout, so velocity (which includes direction) is always changing
Yes — speed is constant at 10 m/s, so velocity must also be constant
Yes — velocity is always 10 m/s regardless of direction
No — the car is accelerating because it is going around a circle

wrong_explanations.1: Speed is scalar and CAN be constant — velocity is vector and changes when direction changes.
wrong_explanations.2: 10 m/s is speed — velocity = 10 m/s north, then 10 m/s northeast, etc. The direction changes.
wrong_explanations.3: Option D is actually CORRECT (circular motion does involve acceleration) — but the reason given (acceleration) is a consequence, not the answer to 'is velocity constant?'. The primary answer is that direction changes.

foundation.common_mistake: Speed is scalar (magnitude only). Velocity is vector (magnitude AND direction). A car travelling in a circle at constant SPEED has changing VELOCITY — direction is always changing. Average speed = total distance ÷ total time (not displacement).

forces/distance-time-graphs.summary: Interpret distance–time graphs and calculate speed from the gradient.

quiz.q: A d–t graph shows a horizontal (flat) line for 5 seconds. What is the object doing?
opts: The object is stationary — no change in distance means zero speed
The object is moving at constant speed — horizontal means steady motion
The object is accelerating — the line is preparing to slope upward
The object has reached maximum speed and is maintaining it

wrong_explanations.1: A horizontal line on a VELOCITY–time graph means constant velocity — but on a DISTANCE–time graph it means zero speed (stationary).
wrong_explanations.2: Flat line = zero gradient = zero speed. Constant speed on a d–t graph is a SLOPED straight line.
wrong_explanations.3: Acceleration on a d–t graph shows as a CURVE (increasing gradient) — not a flat section.

quiz.q: On a distance–time graph, two straight sections are drawn. Section A has gradient 8 m/s and section B has gradient 2 m/s. Which section shows faster motion?
opts: Section A — steeper gradient on a d–t graph means higher speed
Section B — less steep means the object is working harder to maintain motion
They are the same speed — both are straight lines
Section B — a gentler slope indicates faster, more efficient motion

wrong_explanations.1: Gentler slope does not imply harder work — gradient directly equals speed on a d–t graph.
wrong_explanations.2: Both are straight lines (constant speed) but at DIFFERENT speeds — the gradient value gives the speed.
wrong_explanations.3: Gentle slope = smaller gradient = slower speed. Fast motion = steeper slope = larger gradient.

foundation.common_mistake: A FLAT (horizontal) section means the object is STATIONARY — not moving at constant speed. Constant speed gives a diagonal straight line. A steeper line means FASTER speed.

forces/acceleration.summary: Calculate acceleration and use velocity–time graphs to describe and analyse motion.

quiz.q: A car decelerates from 24 m/s to 6 m/s in 9 s. What is the acceleration?
opts: −2 m/s² — a = (6−24)÷9 = −18÷9 = −2 m/s² (negative = deceleration)
2 m/s² — ignoring the sign of deceleration
−3.3 m/s² — a = (6−24)÷(6+3) using wrong time
−18 m/s² — a = v − u without dividing by t

wrong_explanations.1: The magnitude is correct (2 m/s²) but the SIGN matters: deceleration is negative acceleration. a = (v−u)/t = (6−24)/9 = −2 m/s².
wrong_explanations.2: This is not a standard calculation — t = 9 s must be used.
wrong_explanations.3: a = (v−u)/t = (6−24)/9 = −18/9 = −2. Must divide by time.

quiz.q: A v–t graph shows a straight horizontal line at 12 m/s for 6 seconds. What is the distance covered?
opts: 72 m — area = base × height = 6 × 12 = 72 m
2 m — gradient = 12/6 = 2 m/s² (used gradient formula)
0 m — horizontal line means stationary on a v–t graph
12 m — only reading the speed value, not calculating area

wrong_explanations.1: Gradient of a v–t graph gives ACCELERATION — 12/6 = 2 m/s². Distance comes from the AREA.
wrong_explanations.2: A horizontal line on a v–t graph means CONSTANT VELOCITY (not zero) — stationary would be v = 0.
wrong_explanations.3: 12 m/s is the velocity — to get distance, multiply by time: 12 × 6 = 72 m (or calculate area).

foundation.common_mistake: Gradient of a v–t graph = ACCELERATION (not distance or speed). Area under a v–t graph = DISTANCE. This is the opposite of the d–t graph where gradient = speed. Don't confuse the two graphs.

forces/newtons-laws.summary: State and apply Newton's three laws of motion.

quiz.q: A 1500 kg car accelerates at 2 m/s². What is the resultant force?

wrong_explanations.1: F = m ÷ a inverts the formula — F = m × a = 1500 × 2 = 3000 N.
wrong_explanations.2: Adding mass and acceleration gives meaningless units (kg + m/s²).
wrong_explanations.3: There's no factor of 10 in F = ma — this confuses g with acceleration.

quiz.q: A book sits on a table. The book pushes down on the table with force 10 N (its weight). What is the Newton's Third Law reaction to this?
opts: The table pushes UP on the book with 10 N — equal, opposite, on the table (reacting on the book)
The normal contact force from the table pushing up on the book — this balances the weight
The Earth's gravity pulling the book down — the reaction to the normal force
The book pushes down on the Earth through the table with 10 N

wrong_explanations.1: The normal force IS the Newton's Third Law reaction — but the question asks what force it is, and the key is it acts ON THE BOOK FROM THE TABLE. Option B describes it correctly but incorrectly labels it as 'balancing' — N3 pairs don't balance (they're on different objects).
wrong_explanations.2: Earth's gravity on the book is a different force pair entirely (book pulls Earth up / Earth pulls book down). It's not the reaction to the normal contact force.
wrong_explanations.3: The book presses on the table (not directly on Earth) — and the N3 pair to 'book pushes on table' is 'table pushes on book'. The chain to Earth involves more steps.

foundation.common_mistake: Newton's Third Law pairs act on DIFFERENT objects — they cannot cancel. When a horse pulls a cart (action), the cart pulls back on the horse (reaction). These don't cancel because they act on different things. Newton's First Law is about ONE object — don't confuse the two.

forces/stopping-distance-braking.summary: Define stopping distance and explain factors affecting thinking distance and braking distance.

quiz.q: A car's speed doubles from 10 m/s to 20 m/s. How does the braking distance change?
opts: It quadruples — braking distance is proportional to v², so (20/10)² = 4 times longer
It doubles — speed doubles so distance doubles
It increases by 10 m — simple addition
It stays the same — braking distance depends only on road conditions

wrong_explanations.1: Braking distance ∝ v (not v²) would give doubling — but it's ∝ v², so doubling speed multiplies braking distance by 4.
wrong_explanations.2: Braking distance is not fixed — it depends on speed squared, road conditions and braking force.
wrong_explanations.3: Braking distance depends strongly on speed (∝v²) — doubling speed quadruples it, not keeps it constant.

quiz.q: Why does alcohol increase stopping distance?
opts: Alcohol increases reaction time — the driver takes longer to respond, so thinking distance increases
Alcohol weakens muscles — the driver cannot press the brakes as hard, increasing braking distance
Alcohol blurs vision — the driver takes longer to see the hazard and applies brakes later
Alcohol reduces vehicle mass — lighter car has less braking force available

wrong_explanations.1: While impaired vision and muscle weakness may play minor roles, the PRIMARY effect of alcohol on stopping distance is through REACTION TIME (thinking distance component).
wrong_explanations.2: Vision is relevant but the primary mechanism tested at GCSE is the effect on REACTION TIME specifically.
wrong_explanations.3: Alcohol doesn't change vehicle mass — the driver's mass is a tiny fraction of the vehicle.

foundation.common_mistake: Braking distance ∝ v² — doubling speed QUADRUPLES braking distance (not doubles). Thinking distance is proportional to v (doubles when speed doubles). Students often mix up which factor affects which component.

fifas.label: Stopping Distance
fifas.question: A driver has reaction time 0.6 s and is driving at 20 m/s. The braking distance is 40 m. Calculate the total stopping distance.
steps: Stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance; thinking distance = speed × reaction time
Speed = 20 m/s, reaction time = 0.6 s, braking distance = 40 m

quiz.q: A car's speed doubles from 10 m/s to 20 m/s. How does the braking distance change?
opts: It quadruples — braking distance is proportional to v², so (20/10)² = 4 times longer
It doubles — speed doubles so distance doubles
It increases by 10 m — simple addition
It stays the same — braking distance depends only on road conditions

wrong_explanations.1: Braking distance ∝ v (not v²) would give doubling — but it's ∝ v², so doubling speed multiplies braking distance by 4.
wrong_explanations.2: Braking distance is not fixed — it depends on speed squared, road conditions and braking force.
wrong_explanations.3: Braking distance depends strongly on speed (∝v²) — doubling speed quadruples it, not keeps it constant.

quiz.q: Why does alcohol increase stopping distance?
opts: Alcohol increases reaction time — the driver takes longer to respond, so thinking distance increases
Alcohol weakens muscles — the driver cannot press the brakes as hard, increasing braking distance
Alcohol blurs vision — the driver takes longer to see the hazard and applies brakes later
Alcohol reduces vehicle mass — lighter car has less braking force available

wrong_explanations.1: While impaired vision and muscle weakness may play minor roles, the PRIMARY effect of alcohol on stopping distance is through REACTION TIME (thinking distance component).
wrong_explanations.2: Vision is relevant but the primary mechanism tested at GCSE is the effect on REACTION TIME specifically.
wrong_explanations.3: Alcohol doesn't change vehicle mass — the driver's mass is a tiny fraction of the vehicle.
