Explain why worn tyres increase braking distance — reduced tread depth decreases water dispersal, reducing friction. Evaluate the relationship between deceleration and the risk of injury in vehicle collisions.

waves/transverse-longitudinal-waves.summary: Describe transverse and longitudinal waves and give examples of each.

quiz.q: How do particles move in a longitudinal wave compared to the direction of wave travel?
opts: Parallel — particles oscillate back and forth along the same direction as the wave travels
Perpendicular — particles oscillate at right angles to the wave direction
They move with the wave — carried forward as the wave progresses
They remain completely stationary — only energy travels

wrong_explanations.1: PERPENDICULAR oscillation describes TRANSVERSE waves — in longitudinal waves, oscillation is parallel.
wrong_explanations.2: Particles in a wave oscillate about their equilibrium position — they don't travel with the wave. This is the key distinction between wave motion and particle transport.
wrong_explanations.3: Particles do move — they oscillate back and forth. They just don't travel with the wave as a whole.

quiz.q: Why can light travel through a vacuum but sound cannot?
opts: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — needs no medium. Sound is longitudinal and needs particles to compress and rarefy.
Sound travels faster than light and 'uses up' the medium, leaving nothing for light
Light is brighter than sound waves — intensity allows it to travel without a medium
Sound is a transverse wave and cannot travel without a medium; light is longitudinal and can

wrong_explanations.1: Light travels much FASTER than sound (3×10⁸ m/s vs ~340 m/s) — speed is irrelevant to whether a medium is needed.
wrong_explanations.2: Wave intensity is unrelated to whether a medium is required.
wrong_explanations.3: It's the OPPOSITE: sound is LONGITUDINAL (needs medium); light is TRANSVERSE EM wave (no medium needed).

foundation.common_mistake: In a TRANSVERSE wave, the oscillation is PERPENDICULAR to the direction of travel — not parallel. In a LONGITUDINAL wave (like sound), the oscillation is PARALLEL to the direction of travel. Don't confuse the two.

waves/properties-of-waves.summary: Define amplitude, wavelength, frequency, period and wave speed, and use v = fλ.

quiz.q: A wave has frequency 200 Hz. What is its period?
opts: 0.1 s — T = 1/10 of the frequency

wrong_explanations.1: T is not equal to f — they are reciprocals: T = 1/f = 1/200 = 0.005 s.
wrong_explanations.2: T = 1/f not f/10.

quiz.q: A radio wave has wavelength 3 m. What is its frequency? (speed of EM waves = 3 × 10⁸ m/s)
opts: 3 × 10⁸ Hz — frequency equals wave speed for EM waves

wrong_explanations.1: f = v × λ multiplies rather than divides — must use f = v ÷ λ.
wrong_explanations.2: f = λ ÷ v inverts the rearrangement. f = v ÷ λ = 3×10⁸ ÷ 3 = 1×10⁸ Hz.
wrong_explanations.3: Wave speed and frequency are not the same — they are related by v = fλ.

foundation.common_mistake: Amplitude is the distance from the equilibrium (centre) to the crest — NOT from crest to trough (that's double the amplitude). Frequency and period are reciprocals: f = 1/T. Don't confuse wavelength (one full cycle length) with amplitude (height from centre).

fifas.label: Wave Equation
fifas.question: A sound wave has frequency 500 Hz and travels at 340 m/s. Calculate its wavelength.
steps: v = f × λ, so λ = v ÷ f

quiz.q: A wave has frequency 200 Hz. What is its period?
opts: 0.1 s — T = 1/10 of the frequency

wrong_explanations.1: T is not equal to f — they are reciprocals: T = 1/f = 1/200 = 0.005 s.
wrong_explanations.2: T = 1/f not f/10.

opts: 3 × 10⁸ Hz — frequency equals wave speed for EM waves

waves/types-of-em-waves.summary: Describe the electromagnetic spectrum and the properties all EM waves share.

quiz.q: Which EM wave has the highest frequency?
opts: Gamma rays — at the short-wavelength end of the spectrum; highest frequency and energy
Radio waves — longest wavelength means highest frequency
Visible light — humans evolved to see the most frequent EM radiation
Microwaves — used in microwave ovens, must have highest energy

wrong_explanations.1: Longest wavelength = LOWEST frequency (v = fλ — for constant v, f and λ are inversely proportional).
wrong_explanations.2: Visible light is a narrow band in the middle of the spectrum — far from the highest frequency.
wrong_explanations.3: Microwave ovens work by matching the frequency to water molecules' resonant frequency, not because microwaves have the highest energy.

quiz.q: All electromagnetic waves in a vacuum have the same what?
opts: Speed — all travel at 3 × 10⁸ m/s regardless of wavelength or frequency
Wavelength — all EM waves have the same wavelength in vacuum
Frequency — the frequency is constant for all EM waves
Energy — all EM waves carry the same amount of energy

wrong_explanations.1: EM waves vary enormously in wavelength — from >100 m (radio) to <0.01 nm (gamma).
wrong_explanations.2: Frequency varies inversely with wavelength for a given speed — radio (low f) to gamma (high f).
wrong_explanations.3: Energy per photon = hf — higher frequency EM waves carry more energy per photon.

foundation.common_mistake: All EM waves travel at the SAME speed in vacuum (3 × 10⁸ m/s) — they differ only in wavelength and frequency. Higher frequency = shorter wavelength = more energy per photon. Gamma rays are NOT the same as X-rays — they come from different sources (nucleus vs electron deceleration).

waves/properties-em-waves-1.summary: Describe refraction of EM waves and construct ray diagrams at boundaries.

quiz.q: A ray of light passes from air into a glass block. The angle of incidence is 40°. What happens to the ray?
opts: It bends towards the normal — angle of refraction is less than 40°, because light slows down in glass
It bends away from the normal — angle of refraction is greater than 40°
It travels straight through without bending — glass has no effect on direction
It reflects back — light cannot pass from air into glass

wrong_explanations.1: Bending AWAY happens when light goes from glass to air (speeds up). Air → glass means slowing down → bends TOWARDS normal.
wrong_explanations.2: Light is transmitted through glass — it doesn't fully reflect at normal incidence.
wrong_explanations.3: Total internal reflection only occurs when light travels from a denser medium (glass) at an angle exceeding the critical angle — not when going from air into glass.

quiz.q: When light is refracted into a slower medium, which property changes?
opts: Wavelength and speed both decrease — frequency stays constant
Frequency increases — higher frequency in a denser medium
Speed increases — denser medium speeds waves up
All properties change — wavelength, frequency and speed all change

wrong_explanations.1: Frequency is determined by the SOURCE — it doesn't change at a boundary. Speed and wavelength both change (λ = v/f).
wrong_explanations.2: Speed DECREASES in a denser/slower medium — waves slow down, not speed up.
wrong_explanations.3: Frequency is the one property that DOESN'T change during refraction — only speed and wavelength change.

foundation.common_mistake: When light enters glass from air, it SLOWS DOWN and bends TOWARDS the normal (angle of refraction < angle of incidence). When it leaves glass into air, it SPEEDS UP and bends AWAY from the normal. Always identify which medium is faster/slower.
foundation.higher_box: Explain refraction in terms of velocity change: when wave enters denser medium, wavefronts slow and compress, causing a change in direction. Explain total internal reflection (TIR): when angle exceeds critical angle in a denser medium, all light reflects internally. Applications: optical fibres, diamond cut. Calculate critical angle: sin(c) = 1/n.

waves/properties-em-waves-2.summary: Describe hazards of EM radiation and how different waves interact with matter.

quiz.q: Why are X-rays more harmful to living tissue than infrared radiation of the same intensity?
opts: X-rays are ionising — they have enough energy to remove electrons from atoms, damaging DNA. Infrared only heats tissue.
X-rays travel faster than infrared — more collisions with cells per second
Infrared is absorbed at the surface — X-rays penetrate deeper
X-rays have longer wavelength — this allows them to interact with more DNA molecules

wrong_explanations.1: All EM waves travel at the same speed (3×10⁸ m/s) in vacuum — speed doesn't determine hazard.
wrong_explanations.2: While penetration IS a factor in X-ray safety, the fundamental reason X-rays are more harmful is IONISATION — they can break chemical bonds and damage DNA.
wrong_explanations.3: X-rays have SHORTER wavelength than infrared. Shorter wavelength = higher frequency = more energy per photon = more likely to ionise.

quiz.q: A person sunbathes without sunscreen. Which part of sunlight causes the most DNA damage?
opts: Ultraviolet — UV has enough energy to penetrate skin cells and damage DNA, causing mutations
Infrared — warmth from the sun causes DNA to denature
Visible light — the bright light damages cell membranes
All parts equally — sunlight causes damage through all wavelengths together

wrong_explanations.1: Infrared causes thermal effects (sunburn/heat) — but DNA damage specifically comes from UV ionising radiation.
wrong_explanations.2: Visible light at solar intensities can damage the eyes (retina) but doesn't significantly damage skin DNA.
wrong_explanations.3: UV is specifically responsible for DNA damage and skin cancer — this is why SPF sunscreens block UV, not the whole spectrum.

foundation.common_mistake: UV, X-rays and gamma rays are all IONISING — they can damage DNA. Infrared and visible light can cause burns/eye damage but are NOT ionising. The hazard from ionising radiation is cancer and cell damage — not just burns.
foundation.higher_box: Radio waves produced by electrical oscillations — when absorbed they induce AC at the same frequency. Explain how this is used in radio reception. Describe greenhouse effect of atmosphere in terms of IR absorption and re-emission by greenhouse gases. Discuss how changes in climate models affect predictions.

waves/uses-em-waves.summary: Describe the uses of each type of electromagnetic wave and explain why each type is suitable.

quiz.q: Why are microwaves used for satellite communications rather than radio waves?
opts: Microwaves pass through the ionosphere — radio waves reflect off it, preventing them from reaching satellites in orbit
Microwaves are more powerful — they reach greater distances than radio waves
Radio waves are dangerous at high altitude — microwaves are safer for satellite use
Satellites can only detect microwaves — their receivers are not compatible with radio waves

wrong_explanations.1: Power determines signal strength at a given distance — but all EM waves travel at the same speed. The key distinction is IONOSPHERE INTERACTION.
wrong_explanations.2: Safety is not the relevant factor — the ionosphere physically reflects (most) radio waves back to Earth.
wrong_explanations.3: This is backwards — it's the PHYSICS of ionosphere reflection, not receiver incompatibility, that determines which EM type is used.

quiz.q: UV light is used to sterilise medical equipment. Why is UV effective for this?
opts: UV has enough energy to damage DNA in microorganisms — killing or inactivating bacteria and viruses
UV heats the equipment to high temperatures, killing microorganisms by heat
UV is absorbed by metal — heating the equipment surface to kill bacteria
UV converts oxygen to ozone, which then kills bacteria chemically

wrong_explanations.1: UV can cause some warming but it's not the primary mechanism — UV sterilisation works through DNA damage (photochemical action), not significant heating.
wrong_explanations.2: UV sterilisation is used for transparent surfaces, water, and air — metal surfaces are typically sterilised by heat or chemicals.
wrong_explanations.3: UV can produce some ozone, but the primary sterilisation mechanism is direct DNA damage from UV photons — not ozone.

foundation.common_mistake: MRI scanners use RADIO WAVES (not X-rays) — they are safe for soft tissue imaging with no ionising radiation. X-rays are used for imaging BONE and dense structures. Also: microwaves for SATELLITE communication (pass through ionosphere); radio waves for BROADCAST (reflect off ionosphere).

magnetism/poles-of-a-magnet.summary: Describe the properties of magnets, distinguish permanent from induced magnetism, and describe magnetic forces.

quiz.q: A steel paper clip is brought near a bar magnet. Why is the paper clip attracted?
opts: The steel becomes an induced magnet — the end nearest the magnet becomes opposite in polarity, so they attract
Steel is made from iron which is naturally magnetic — it has its own permanent field that attracts
The paper clip gains an electric charge from the magnetic field — opposite charges attract
The magnetic field passes through the paper clip, pulling it forward

wrong_explanations.1: Steel is a MAGNETIC MATERIAL (ferromagnetic) but not permanently magnetised until it is magnetised. The attraction is due to INDUCED MAGNETISM from the bar magnet.
wrong_explanations.2: Magnetism and electric charge are separate — magnetic fields don't charge objects with static electricity.
wrong_explanations.3: Magnetic forces act on magnetic materials — they don't 'push' the clip forward. The attraction is between the magnetic poles of the induced magnet and the bar magnet.

quiz.q: A fridge magnet and a compass are both magnets. What is the key difference between them regarding their magnetism?
opts: Fridge magnet: permanent — retains field indefinitely. Compass: also permanent — compass needle must always point north reliably
Fridge magnet: temporary — it loses magnetism each day. Compass: permanent
Fridge magnet: stronger field — household magnets are more powerful than compass needles
Fridge magnet: induced. Compass: permanent — all small magnets are induced magnets

wrong_explanations.1: Both ARE permanent magnets — but the question is testing understanding that both fridge magnets and compass needles are permanent (made from hard magnetic materials).
wrong_explanations.2: Strength varies between magnets but strength is not the distinguishing feature the question asks about.
wrong_explanations.3: Both the fridge magnet and compass needle are PERMANENT magnets — they must retain their magnetism to function.

foundation.common_mistake: Only IRON, STEEL, NICKEL and COBALT are magnetic materials. Aluminium and copper are NOT attracted to magnets. Permanent magnets (steel) retain magnetism. Induced magnets (iron) lose it when the field is removed.

magnetism/magnetic-fields.summary: Describe magnetic fields, draw field line patterns and explain how they show force and direction.

quiz.q: A student plots a magnetic field using iron filings around a bar magnet. The filings cluster densely near the poles. What does this show?
opts: The field is strongest near the poles — closely spaced field lines indicate greater magnetic force in that region
The poles have more mass — iron filings are attracted to mass, not field strength
The poles are the least magnetised parts — filings avoid the strong field regions
Iron filings cluster near poles due to gravity — the poles are at the ends

wrong_explanations.1: Iron filings are attracted to magnetic fields — they cluster where the field is STRONGEST (at poles), not due to mass.
wrong_explanations.2: Dense filings = strong field = more attraction. Less magnetised areas would have fewer filings.
wrong_explanations.3: The magnet is horizontal in typical demonstrations — gravity doesn't explain end-clustering.

quiz.q: Which direction do magnetic field lines point outside a bar magnet?
opts: From the north pole to the south pole — field lines exit the north pole and enter the south pole
From south to north — the south pole is the 'positive' terminal
In random directions — field lines don't have a set direction
From both poles outward — field lines never enter the magnet

wrong_explanations.1: Convention states field lines go from N to S — the direction a free north pole would move.
wrong_explanations.2: Field lines have a specific direction defined by convention — from N to S.
wrong_explanations.3: Field lines form closed loops — they exit the north pole, curve around, and re-enter at the south pole.

foundation.common_mistake: Field lines go from NORTH to SOUTH outside the magnet — this shows the direction a north pole would move. Field lines NEVER cross. Closer field lines = STRONGER field — not weaker. The Earth's geographic north pole is actually a magnetic SOUTH pole (that's why compass north is attracted to it).

quiz.q: A student plots a magnetic field using iron filings around a bar magnet. The filings cluster densely near the poles. What does this show?
opts: The field is strongest near the poles — closely spaced field lines indicate greater magnetic force in that region
The poles have more mass — iron filings are attracted to mass, not field strength
The poles are the least magnetised parts — filings avoid the strong field regions
Iron filings cluster near poles due to gravity — the poles are at the ends

wrong_explanations.1: Iron filings are attracted to magnetic fields — they cluster where the field is STRONGEST (at poles), not due to mass.
wrong_explanations.2: Dense filings = strong field = more attraction. Less magnetised areas would have fewer filings.
wrong_explanations.3: The magnet is horizontal in typical demonstrations — gravity doesn't explain end-clustering.

quiz.q: Which direction do magnetic field lines point outside a bar magnet?
opts: From the north pole to the south pole — field lines exit the north pole and enter the south pole
From south to north — the south pole is the 'positive' terminal
In random directions — field lines don't have a set direction
From both poles outward — field lines never enter the magnet

wrong_explanations.1: Convention states field lines go from N to S — the direction a free north pole would move.
wrong_explanations.2: Field lines have a specific direction defined by convention — from N to S.
wrong_explanations.3: Field lines form closed loops — they exit the north pole, curve around, and re-enter at the south pole.

Closer field lines = STRONGER field — not weaker. The Earth's geographic north pole is actually a magnetic SOUTH pole (that's why compass north is attracted to it).

magnetism/electromagnetism.summary: Describe the magnetic field around a current-carrying wire, solenoid and electromagnet.

quiz.q: Why is an iron core used in an electromagnet instead of a steel core?
opts: Iron is a soft magnetic material — it quickly loses magnetism when the current is switched off, so the electromagnet can be turned off easily
Iron is harder than steel — it is more durable and resists damage
Iron has higher electrical conductivity than steel — more current flows through it
Steel would make the magnet too strong — iron limits the field to a safe level

wrong_explanations.1: Steel is harder mechanically, but in magnetism 'hard' means it RETAINS magnetism — which is not wanted for a switchable electromagnet.
wrong_explanations.2: The iron core is not in the electrical circuit — it is inside the coil. Current flows through the wire coil, not the core.
wrong_explanations.3: The goal is NOT to limit strength — electromagnets are made as strong as possible. The issue is being able to switch off.

quiz.q: What happens to the strength of an electromagnet's field when the current is doubled?
opts: The field doubles in strength — magnetic field strength is directly proportional to current
The field halves — more current creates resistance which reduces the field
The field stays the same — only the number of turns affects field strength
The field quadruples — field strength increases with current squared

wrong_explanations.1: The magnetic field of an electromagnet is proportional to current (and to number of turns) — it doesn't reduce when current increases.
wrong_explanations.2: BOTH current and number of turns affect field strength. Doubling current doubles the field.
wrong_explanations.3: Field strength is directly proportional to current (not current squared). Doubling current → double field.

foundation.common_mistake: An electromagnet uses IRON (soft) — not steel (hard). Iron demagnetises when the current is switched off. Steel would retain magnetism. Increasing CURRENT or NUMBER OF TURNS both increase the strength of an electromagnet's field.
foundation.higher_box: The motor effect: a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force. F = BIl (force = flux density × current × length). Fleming's Left-Hand Rule: thumb = force direction, index = field, middle finger = current. Applications: electric motors (rotating coil in field). Induced EMF (generator effect).

higher.higher_box: Describe the motor effect: a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force F = BIl. Apply Fleming's Left-Hand Rule to determine force direction. Explain how the motor effect is used in loudspeakers. Describe electromagnetic induction (generator effect): a conductor moving in a magnetic field induces an EMF — Fleming's Right-Hand Rule gives induced current direction.

forces/momentum.summary: Define momentum, calculate it using p = mv and apply conservation of momentum to collisions.

quiz.q: A 0.5 kg ball moving at 8 m/s collides with a stationary 1.5 kg ball. They stick together. What is their combined velocity?
opts: 4 m/s — using only the moving ball's mass: 4/0.5 = 8... wrong rearrangement
8 m/s — velocity is conserved, not momentum
0 m/s — the stationary ball stops the moving one

wrong_explanations.1: p = 4 kg m/s. After: (0.5+1.5)v = 4; 2v = 4; v = 2 m/s — not 4 (uses wrong total mass).
wrong_explanations.2: Velocity is NOT conserved — MOMENTUM is conserved. Speed always decreases when objects stick together.
wrong_explanations.3: The stationary ball doesn't have enough momentum to stop the moving one — momentum must balance.

quiz.q: Why do modern cars have crumple zones at the front?
opts: They increase the time of the collision, reducing the peak force on passengers for the same change in momentum
They reduce the total momentum change — less deceleration occurs
They absorb all kinetic energy — no force is transmitted to the passenger compartment
They make the car lighter — less mass means less momentum and a gentler stop

wrong_explanations.1: The momentum change (impulse) is the same — the car still goes from moving to stationary. Crumple zones increase TIME, not reduce the impulse.
wrong_explanations.2: Crumple zones don't absorb ALL kinetic energy without any force — they spread the energy absorption over a longer time to reduce peak force.
wrong_explanations.3: Crumple zones add mass (metal structure) — the mass reduction argument is not the mechanism.

higher.common_mistake: Momentum is a VECTOR — direction matters. When objects move in opposite directions, one momentum is negative. In conservation problems: always define a positive direction first, then assign signs accordingly. Total momentum is ZERO before an explosion (object at rest), so the two parts fly off with equal and opposite momenta.

fifas.label: Conservation of Momentum
fifas.question: A 600 kg car travelling at 15 m/s collides with a stationary 400 kg car. They stick together. Find their velocity after the collision.
steps: Total p before = total p after: m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁+m₂)v

magnetism/flemings-left-hand-rule.summary: Apply Fleming's Left-Hand Rule to predict force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.

quiz.q: A horizontal wire carries current to the right in a downward magnetic field. Using Fleming's Left-Hand Rule, in which direction is the force on the wire?
opts: Out of the page (towards the observer) — field down, current right, force out
Into the page — field down, current right, force in
Upward — force is opposite to the field direction
To the left — force is opposite to current direction

wrong_explanations.1: Apply the rule carefully: first finger DOWN (field), second finger RIGHT (current), thumb points OUT OF PAGE — not into.
wrong_explanations.2: Force is perpendicular to BOTH the field and current — not opposite to either individually.
wrong_explanations.3: Force is perpendicular to current, not opposite to it — F = BIl gives a force at right angles to both.

quiz.q: How can the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field be reversed?
opts: Reverse the current direction OR reverse the magnetic field direction — either change reverses the force
Increase the current magnitude — larger current reverses the force direction
Move the conductor parallel to the field — this reverses the force
Reduce the magnetic flux density to zero then increase it again

wrong_explanations.1: Changing the MAGNITUDE of current changes the FORCE SIZE — not its direction.
wrong_explanations.2: A conductor parallel to the field experiences ZERO force — not reversed force.
wrong_explanations.3: Reducing B to zero means no force — increasing it again gives the same direction as before.

higher.common_mistake: Fleming's LEFT-hand rule is for MOTORS (force on current in a field). Fleming's RIGHT-hand rule is for GENERATORS (induced current from motion in a field). Students often use the wrong hand. Remember: Left = motor effect (current → force). Right = generator effect (motion → current).

fifas.label: Motor Effect Force
fifas.question: A wire of length 0.05 m carries a current of 4 A in a magnetic field of flux density 2 T (perpendicular to the wire). Calculate the force on the wire.

magnetism/electric-motors.summary: Explain how the motor effect causes a coil to rotate in an electric motor.

quiz.q: Why does a DC motor need a split-ring commutator?
opts: It reverses the current in the coil every half turn — ensuring the forces always act to rotate the coil in the same direction
It increases the magnetic field strength — making the motor more powerful
It converts AC supply to DC for the motor coil
It connects the motor to the power supply — without it no current would flow

wrong_explanations.1: The commutator doesn't affect field strength — it only switches current direction.
wrong_explanations.2: A commutator converts DC to pulsed DC in the coil — it's not an AC-to-DC converter (that's a rectifier).
wrong_explanations.3: The brushes connect to the supply — the commutator specifically reverses current direction at the right moment.

quiz.q: An electric vehicle uses regenerative braking. What energy transfer occurs?
opts: Kinetic energy → electrical energy — the motor runs in reverse as a generator, charging the battery
Electrical energy → kinetic energy — the motor accelerates the car to a stop
Chemical energy → thermal energy — brakes convert stored energy to heat
Kinetic energy → thermal energy — friction brakes absorb kinetic energy as heat

wrong_explanations.1: Accelerating the car to a stop makes no sense — regenerative braking decelerates by extracting energy from the motion.
wrong_explanations.2: Conventional friction brakes do convert KE to thermal — but regenerative braking specifically avoids this by converting to electrical energy instead.
wrong_explanations.3: Both conventional braking (KE → thermal) and regenerative braking (KE → electrical) happen simultaneously in many EVs — but regenerative braking specifically refers to the electrical recovery.

higher.common_mistake: The SPLIT-RING COMMUTATOR swaps the current direction every half turn — this is what makes a DC motor spin continuously rather than oscillating. Without the commutator, the coil would rock back and forth. The commutator is unique to DC motors — AC motors use a different mechanism.
